what: moat appeared 5x, differentiator 2x, commoditizes 1x in shipped brief
why: reads like MBA/VC pitch deck, not builder-to-builder
outcome: MBA vocab banned in brief-blueprint + tests/golden-format.sh regex gate

what: Canva isn't adding AI image generation. They're changing what it means to design.
why: [Not X, it's Y] inversion — #1 AI tell
outcome: zero-tolerance banned, regex-enforced, blueprint instructs to use direct declarative or parallel structure with different subject

what: 1227 words felt thin
why: briefs optimize for Aayush's learning, not brevity
outcome: floor raised to 2500 words, no cap, go deeper when topic demands

what: 7 sentences over 22 words
why: hard to parse on first read
outcome: 22-word sentence cap in blueprint

what: ## Sources footer at bottom
why: noise — reader skims past it, loses the argument
outcome: footer banned, inline citations mandatory (≥3 markdown links)

what: lacks explain-the-mechanism
why: reads as news recap, not learning
outcome: every claim must answer: why-now + causal chain forward + mental-model shift

what: all 3 options under 1100 chars (856, 948, 1015)
why: below top-5 performer floor — too thin for hook+proof+implication+closer
outcome: floor raised to 1300 chars (top-5 avg excluding announcement = 1368)

what: 'moat' was banned in posts-gate
why: wrong call — Aayush uses 'moats' 4x in his own top performers
outcome: unbanned for posts (stays banned in briefs — different audiences)

what: 'IMO' and 'I doubt' missing from voice
why: these are his signature hedge markers
outcome: added as allowed voice anchors in post-blueprint

what: unicode-bold formatting (𝟰𝟬%, 𝗜𝗦, 𝟵𝟬%) not documented
why: top-1 post (392 eng) uses it — the Aayush signature that survives LinkedIn's lack of markdown
outcome: codified in blueprint with Mathematical Bold block codepoints (U+1D400-U+1D7FF). 1-2 per post max

what: P.S./P.P.S. closer with 👇🏻 image pointer
why: top-1 and top-5 both use this pattern
outcome: codified as reach-for on vulnerable/contrarian posts

what: short fragment paragraphs ('That's distribution.') not encouraged
why: today's shipped post and top performers use them as rhythm device
outcome: added as allowed pattern in blueprint

what: contrast labels (AI First: / AI Native:) pattern
why: top-5 post uses it for binary framing
outcome: codified as rhythm device

what: parallel imperatives ('Kill the constraint. Kill the process.')
why: top-5 post rhythm device
outcome: codified as rhythm device

what: era-reframe pattern ('2023 strategy on 2026 timelines')
why: today's shipped post uses it — rhetorical device naming strategy by vintage year
outcome: codified as allowed pattern

what: ellipsis hook ('AI startups have 12 months before they die…')
why: today's shipped post uses it — trailing ellipsis adds stakes without full sentence
outcome: codified as Pattern D variant

what: lowercase rhetorical opener ('what are you building that only YOU can build?')
why: today's shipped post uses lowercase 'what' for personal/rhetorical questions
outcome: allowed opening pattern

what: ALL-CAPS one-word emphasis (YOU)
why: used once/post max for vocal stress — today's post + top performers
outcome: allowed rhythm device

what: 'It's like buying a coffee for $5...' analogy in Option 2
why: #1 LLM tell — 'It's like X' declarative analogy
outcome: zero tolerance, regex-enforced in posts-gate.sh

what: zero hashtags policy (was max 3 topic-exact)
why: top performers have zero, bottom performers often have blocks
outcome: tightened to zero

what: regex [Not X, it's Y] was false-flagging parallel comparisons
why: Aayush's 'Customer support logs aren't a moat. Real-time workflow data... is.' uses different subject — legitimate pattern
outcome: regex tightened to same-subject-only in posts-gate.sh

what: creatives were austere (single slate accent on off-white)
why: need real color theory, not decoration
outcome: 5-mood palette system (Violet/Indigo/Emerald/Coral/Ink) with 60-30-10 rule in color-theory.md

what: '@aayush-maheshwari · presidential briefing' footer
why: branding for a personal post reads wrong
outcome: footer now LinkedIn icon (SVG, #0a66c2) + 'aayush-maheshwari' + date

what: copy on creatives used banned MBA vocab (commodity, moat, application layer)
why: same voice rules apply to creative copy
outcome: design-council SKILL.md now references plain-english-rules.md as mandatory read before any copy

what: user: 'right now what you're giving me is stupid anyway' + request for blueprint over hard gates
why: reactive regex-patching has been treating symptoms, not learning
outcome: shift to blueprint-primary architecture. Voice truth in config/*-blueprint.md. Gates stay as universal safety net. Feedback log drives evolution

what: generated posts had zero first-person observer voice
why: posts used 'The founders winning...' / 'Most teams are still...' — generic thought leadership, not Aayush speaking from his own vantage point. He writes 'Every week I watch...', 'Every team I talk to...', 'I've been thinking about...'
outcome: posts v2 rewritten with first-person observer in every option. Blueprint must require at least 1 first-person present-tense observation per post.

what: generated posts had zero hedge markers
why: IMO, I doubt, I think, tbh appear naturally in Aayush's top posts and signal humility+conviction. Pipeline wrote none. Reads like a confident guru, not a practitioner.
outcome: posts v2 include IMO in options 1 and 2, I think in option 3. Blueprint: hedge marker required in every post — at least one of IMO/I think/I doubt/tbh.

what: generated posts had no contrast labels or recap tags
why: Aayush's #5 post uses 'That's distribution.', 'That's proprietary data.', 'That's the third.' as one-liner recap tags after each pillar. His reference post uses 'That's AI Native.' style contrast labels. Pipeline wrote long paragraphs instead.
outcome: posts v2 use 'That's not AI-powered. That's AI Native.' / 'That's the thing competitors can't copy.' / contrast labels throughout. Blueprint: each post must have at least 1 contrast label or 'That's X.' recap tag.

what: generated posts used long compound sentences instead of fragment paragraphs
why: 'When your deployment pipeline runs through Claude Code skills, when your testing gets automated by agents, when code reviews happen through LLM-assisted workflows, suddenly...' — this is consultant deck language. Aayush writes one idea per line, short fragments.
outcome: posts v2 use fragment paragraphs throughout. Blueprint: max 1 compound sentence per paragraph block. Stacked single-idea lines are the signature format.

what: user: 'You've generated shit LinkedIn posts today, is this how my old posts look or the creator posts I shared with you?'
why: voice blueprint exists but model ignored it — wrote in generic LinkedIn thought leadership voice instead. No first-person, no hedge markers, no contrast labels, no fragment paragraphs. Sounds like AI advice, not Aayush.
outcome: root cause: model treating post-blueprint.md as a checklist to comply with rather than a voice to embody. Need stronger voice-matching instruction and mandatory self-check against reference post before submitting.

what: personal-story hooks avg 83 eng vs contrarian 37 / data 35 / other 27 — confirmed from full 25-post history
why: Aayush's top post (305 eng) opens 'I quit my job with zero backup plan.' Top 6 posts by engagement all have first-person moment or personal story arc. Contrarian works (67 eng) but is ceiling-capped vs. personal story which can spike 4-5x
outcome: blueprint update: Vulnerable Victor / personal-story style is TIER 1 — use whenever aayush-experiences.md has a matching entry. Personal-I observer ('Every week I watch...', 'Every team I talk to...') is TIER 2. Pure contrarian/data is TIER 3. Daily options should attempt TIER 1 before falling back.

what: brief writing doesn't match newsletters Aayush reads (AI Daily Brief, The Signal)
why: brief reads like a consulting report — no attribution after claims, no mini-stories about specific people, no first-person narrator. AI Daily Brief attributes every claim to a named person inline. The Signal tells mini-stories: 'Eric is an SMB AE at Zendesk. He has 1,800 accounts.' Our brief says 'companies are adopting this'
outcome: added config/brief-examples/ with style anchors from both newsletters. write-briefing now reads these FIRST. added STYLE REFERENCE section to writing prompt with 7 concrete rules matching newsletter style

what: top creator post examples and blueprint not wired into write-posts skill
why: Aayush shared creator-posts.md and top-creator-blueprint.md months ago. pipeline wasn't reading them. model was writing generic content instead of following the emotional arc (personal moment → specific truth → implication → conviction) that top creator posts demonstrate
outcome: added config/creator-posts.md and config/top-creator-blueprint.md. write-posts now reads these FIRST before rules. added voice self-check to prompt: model must verify first-person, hedge markers, contrast labels, fragment paragraphs before submitting

what: root cause of persistent voice failures: rules without examples
why: both write-briefing and write-posts had 500+ lines of rules but almost no actual examples of what good writing looks like. models learn voice from examples, not instructions. every fix has been adding more rules — none of them stick
outcome: architecture shift: examples first, rules second. both skills now lead with style references. newsletter excerpts and creator posts are loaded before any rule file

what: pipeline hook was 'Every team i talk to says they deployed Claude Code' — aayush changed to 'Everyone has Claude Code. Almost no one is using it right.'
why: personal-observer hook ('every team i talk to') is TIER 2. the better hook is a direct contrarian statement — shorter, broader claim, lands before the fold. 'Everyone has X. Almost no one is using it right.' is a clean 2-line binary that works as a standalone unit.
outcome: add 'Two-line binary' as a standalone hook pattern to blueprint: '[Universal claim]. [Inversion or gap].' — works better than observer hook when the gap/contrast is the whole point. Contrarian Truth (Pattern A) can be expressed as a 2-line binary, not just a single declaration.

what: pipeline had no bridge sentence between hook and story. aayush added 'I see it across my network, teams shipping with identical tools, but getting wildly different outcomes.' and 'The difference lies in what happens when your Claude conversation ends.'
why: the hook grabs — but readers need a 1-sentence bridge that (a) grounds the claim in aayush's actual vantage point and (b) names the specific mechanism or gap. 'The difference lies in X' is a bridge device that transitions from claim to proof without re-stating the hook.
outcome: add 'bridge sentence' as a required structural device between hook and story: one sentence that (a) places aayush as an observer ('I see it across my network', 'Every team I talk to', 'I watch this happen') and (b) names the specific gap or mechanism. Pipeline was jumping straight from hook to story — the bridge is what makes the reader feel the problem before seeing the proof.

what: pipeline wrote 'He built telemetry that logs every Claude Code interaction. Every pattern analyzed. Every skill saved to a shared repository. Every engineer, designer, and PM in the same system.' as one block. aayush broke each line into its own paragraph.
why: stacking short fragments one-per-line creates visual breathing room and rhythm — each line becomes its own beat. a block of 4 short sentences feels like a list trying to be prose. single-line fragments feel like punchlines.
outcome: update blueprint: when listing consequences or actions in sequence, default to one-per-line paragraph breaks. Never stack 3+ short sentences in the same paragraph block. If the sentences are all under 10 words, break them apart.

what: aayush added '(using claude code is not a moat 🤷🏻‍♂️)' as an inline parenthetical aside after 'The tools were identical'
why: parenthetical asides with emoji inject personality and pre-empt the obvious objection in a casual, self-aware way. signals that the author knows what the reader is thinking. not a disclaimer — a wink.
outcome: add 'parenthetical personality aside' as a valid voice device: wrap a casual observation or objection-preemption in parens + 1 emoji. Use once per post max. Works best mid-post after a data point. Example: '(using claude code is not a moat 🤷🏻‍♂️)'. Different from body emoji — this is inline with the sentence, not on its own line.

what: pipeline closing question: 'what does your team's AI memory look like after 12 months of compounding?' — aayush changed to 'What does your team remember from last sprint's Claude Code usage?'
why: aayush's version is more specific (last sprint, not 12 months), more present-tense, and more direct as a self-check. '12 months of compounding' is aspirational-abstract. 'last sprint' is concrete and immediate — forces a real answer, not a hypothetical one.
outcome: closing question guidance: prefer present-tense specific over future-abstract. 'What did you do last week' beats 'What will you do in 12 months'. Make the question answerable right now, not someday. Aayush's questions almost always have a specific timeframe ('last sprint', 'this quarter') or a specific thing ('what does your team remember').

what: aayush added 'p.p.s. at Atlan we've been building this layer for a while. happy to compare notes with anyone doing the same.' — this was not in the pipeline output
why: personal company context as a p.p.s. closes the post with a genuine CTA that doesn't feel like lead gen. 'happy to compare notes' is credibility + invitation without being salesy. grounds the post in aayush's actual work.
outcome: add 'Atlan context p.p.s.' as a valid CTA pattern for posts where Atlan is building in the same space as the theme. When the post topic overlaps with what Atlan does (AI infrastructure, data, agents, ops), add a p.p.s. that mentions 'at Atlan we [1-line description]' + soft invitation. Pipeline should flag when Atlan context is relevant.

what: pipeline p.s. read 'the number that hit me hardest: 100% of Intercom's engineers, designers, and PMs ship through Claude Code now.' — aayush changed to 'the detail that hit this home — at Intercom, designers and PMs now merge PRs through Claude Code.'
why: pipeline p.s. repeated a stat already implied in the post body. aayush's p.s. named a specific concrete detail (designers and PMs merging PRs) that changes what the number means. 'hit this home' is also softer than 'hit me hardest' — less clickbait, more honest.
outcome: p.s. guidance: don't repeat a stat already covered in the body. use the p.s. to land one new specific detail that reframes what the reader just read. the detail should be surprising or concrete in a way the body didn't have room for.
